texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The world's arid and semi-arid zones are already faced with massive water scarcity, which is prospected to increase in the upcoming decades. Therefore the demand of desalination technologies is increasing rapidly. Today more than 50 × 10 6 m 3 of fresh water is produced by industrial desalination processes [1]. In remote areas, conventional desalination processes cannot be applied, due to insufficient infrastructure and energy supply. The development of medium size, autonomous and robust desalination units is needed to establish an independent water supply in this areas. This is the motivation for research on alternative desalination processes. Membrane distillation (MD) seems to fit the specific requirements very well. It is a thermal membrane process that can be driven with any low-grade heat source. The robust membrane allows to follow alternating operation conditions given by, e.g. regenerative energy sources like solar energy.

texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation (MD) is considered a promising technology for desalination applications. Usually, the investigations stated in MD literature cover lab-scale experiments to analyse the technology's potential. This work is focussed on experimental studies on full scale spiral wound MD-modules with a membrane surface area of 5-14m 2 . Module technology and module fabrication are introduced, as well as the fully automated performance test facility and the characterisation procedures. Statements regarding module fabrication quality are given by a comparison of a four module production charge. The experimental results consider module output rate and specific energy consumption for a broad variety of module operation points. The interaction of feed flow rate, temperature levels, feed water salinity and geometrical module design parameters are quantified and discussed. Special attention is given to the influence of salinity on module performance. The salt rejection rate throughout all experiments is very high and almost independent from any studied operational parameters. Finally, a preliminary approach shows the feasibility of high purity distillate production with the spiral wound modules. In a emulated double stage process the distillate conductivity was dropped down to 0.19 𝛍 S/cm.

texts.text: 1.1. Membrane distillation technologies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In MD the vapour pressure difference across a microporous, hydrophobic membrane forces the vapour to permeate through the open membrane pores. In the MD technologies called direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD), permeate gap membrane distillation (PGMD) and air gap membrane distillation (AGMD) the vapour pressure difference is established by means of a temperature difference. In sweeping gas membrane distillation (SGMD) and vacuum membrane distillation (VMD) the vapour pressure differenceisestablishedbyloweringtheconcentrationofthepermeating substance directly on the product side. Summarizing articles on the concept of MD are given by, e.g. Loyd and Lawson [2-4] and Drioli [5].

texts.orig: /Delta1h v represents the enthalpy of evaporation of water and /NAK M the membrane thermal conductivity. Due to the temperature profile from the bulk streams to the boundary layers the interfacial temperature difference ( T 1 -T 0 ) is usually lower than the bulk stream temperature difference ( TH -TC ). The value of this so called temperature polarisation effect is influenced by the hydrodynamic conditions in the evaporator and condenser channels. To lower the polarisation effect is one of the main issues to optimize MD module design. Spacer materials are used to reduce temperature polarisation by introducing turbulences. To improve the ratio of pressure drop and temperature polarisation by finding optimal geometries for spacer materials, experimental studies and CFD analyses were performed by several researchers [11-13]. The determination of adequate correlations for the description of the hydrodynamic boundary layer is a key challenge in mathematical MD modelling. Several correlations are introduced in [5,9,14,15].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 4 shows the channel arrangement for a PGMD module. Cold feed water enters the condenser channel and gains heat to approximately 73 ◦ C by internal heat recovery. An external heat source (e.g. solar collector) heats the feed water up to 80 ◦ C. The hot feed water flows through the evaporator channel in counter current direction and exits the module with 27 ◦ C at the evaporator outlet. An almost constant temperature difference is established throughout the entire membrane surface area comparable with the temperature profile in a counter current flow heat exchanger. Water vapour passes through the membrane and condenses in the distillate channel. The latent and sensible heat is transferred through the condenser foil to preheat the feed water in the condenser channel. Pure distillate exits the module at the distillate outlet.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The modules are fabricated using a membrane, a condenser foil and different spacer materials on rolls. For module fabrication a winding machine was developed. The material coils are placed inside the machine. The layers are aligned with guiding rollers and brought together at a motor driven main spindle. To ensure a well-defined production process each material spindle is equipped with a controlled brake. Here the pretensioning of each layer can be precisely adjusted. Fig. 6 shows a photograph of the winding machine.

texts.orig: In order to evaluate module and process performance a specialised test facility was developed. The hydraulic schematic is given in Fig. 8. The modules operating point can be defined by controlling the main influencing parameters: condenser inlet temperature, evaporator inlet temperature, feed flow rate and feed water salinity. The salty feed water is pumped out of a 300 l-storage tank(1). A 0.5 mm-poresizemechanicalfilter(2)preventsthemodule from particles. The feed water is cooled in a heat exchanger (4) byusingthelaboratorycoolingwatercircuit(11).Anyrequiredcondenser inlet temperature can be set by controlling a motor valve (10) in the cooling loop. After passing through the modules condenser channel (5) the preheated water gains external heat in a heat exchanger (6). The evaporator inlet temperature is controlled bya3-phasepowerswitch,regulatinganelectricalheatingelement (12). After passing the evaporator channel the saltwater goes back in the storage tank for recirculation. The distillate is temporarily stored in a separate tank (7). If the filling level of the distillate tank exceeds a certain threshold, the distillate is pumped back in the storage automatically (8) to avoid a raising feed water salinity.

After passing the evaporator channel the saltwater goes back in the storage tank for recirculation. The distillate is temporarily stored in a separate tank (7). If the filling level of the distillate tank exceeds a certain threshold, the distillate is pumped back in the storage automatically (8) to avoid a raising feed water salinity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The output rate significantly increases with the feed flow rate and ranges from 10 to 25kg/h. Higher flow rates result in an increase in thermal energy input. The increase of the total thermal capacity entering the channels with an increasing feed flow rate, results in an increase of the bulk temperature difference across the membrane. According to Eq. (1) this effect leads to a higher driving force. A second effect of an increasing feed flow rate is the impact on the hydrodynamic conditions in the flow channels. The temperature polarisation is reduced by an enhanced heat transfer in the limiting thermal boundary layers, thus a higher interfacial temperature difference is reached. The specific energy consumption raises with feed flow rate and ranges from 130 to 207 kWh/m 3 . In Eq. (5), the factors /Delta1 T , ˙ m feed and ˙ V dist increase. The higher thermal energy input and the enhancement of the heat transfer are not sufficient to rise up the driving forces accordingly. From an energetic point of view the higher total energy demand is not completely compensated by higher output rates. The internal heat recovery becomes less efficient. The pressure curve reaches pci =0.7bar at a flow rate of 500 kg/h.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An increase of the membrane surface area does not have a high influence on the total distillate output rates, but it significantly lowers the specific energy consumption of the module; e.g. at ˙ m feed = 400kg/h an increase of the membrane area from 5 to 14m 2 results in an increase of distillate output of only 8.5% whereas there is a 57.1% decrease in energy consumption. In general, an increase of membrane area results in a smaller temperature difference across the membrane, similar to heat exchangers with more surface area. The process driving force and therefore the specific output rate decreases. The increase in effective membrane surface area is compensated by a reduced specific output rate. Thus, the influence of the membrane surface area on the total output rate is low. However, producing the same total output with a lower temperaturedifference, the external heat demand drops proportionally to the change in /Delta1 T .

texts.orig: 3.5. Distillate quality achieved with spiral wound MD-modules
texts.text: 3.5. Distillate quality achieved with spiral wound MD-modules

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MD is well known for its high process rejection rates, proven in a broad variety of lab-scale experiments. To prove functionality and quality for this full scale spiral wound module technology, investigations on distillate quality have been performed. Since the pressure level in the condenser and evaporator channel is higher than in the distillate channel, any defect or imperfection would result in a significant increase of salt content in the distillate, electrical conductivity, respectively. The distillate conductivity was measured to evaluate achievable distillate purity and to verify proper module function. The conductivity results from the 10m 2 -module (design #2) used throughout all experiments and in addition from a 14 m 2 -module (design #3) are given in Fig. 15.

texts.orig: At the beginning of the experiment the conductivity level dropped rapidly. This is due to the very high sensitivity of the permeate conductivity to impurities, especially at the given very low conductivity level. Any inevitable surface contamination of the materials from production processes (material delivery conditions and winding process) is getting flushed out of the permeate channel during the first hours of operation. After 1 h of operation the conductivity already reached a value of /DC4 feed =0.36 𝛍 S/cm. After additional 2 h of operation a stable value at approximately /DC4 feed =0.19 𝛍 S/cm was reached.

This is due to the very high sensitivity of the permeate conductivity to impurities, especially at the given very low conductivity level. Any inevitable surface contamination of the materials from production processes (material delivery conditions and winding process) is getting flushed out of the permeate channel during the first hours of operation. After 1 h of operation the conductivity already reached a value of /DC4 feed =0.36 𝛍 S/cm. After additional 2 h of operation a stable value at approximately /DC4 feed =0.19 𝛍 S/cm was reached.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A spiral wound PGMD-module for solar desalination applications with 5-14m 2 effective membrane area has been developed by Fraunhofer ISE. The module fabrication including winding with a self developed winding machine, potting and final assembly was performed on site. More than 130 modules have already been fabricated and characterised in special automated test facilities.
texts.text: A spiral wound PGMD-module for solar desalination applications with 5-14m 2 effective membrane area has been developed by Fraunhofer ISE. The module fabrication including winding with a self developed winding machine, potting and final assembly was performed on site. More than 130 modules have already been fabricated and characterised in special automated test facilities.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thereproducibility of the production was shown by comparison of the measurements of a charge of four similar modules. A very small variance was determined. With usual operation conditions the output rate was between 10 and 25 kg/h with a specific energy consumption of 130-207kWh/m 3 . All results showed a strong influence of the feed flow rate on the module performance due to the changing bulk temperature difference and hydrodynamics.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The reduction of the vapour pressure with salinity reduces the process driving force. The feed water salinity is considered one of the important influencing parameters for the spiral wound module concept. Excessive saltwater measurements with feed water salinities up to S =105g/kg were performed to get quantitative results for the relation of feed flow rate, condenser inlet temperature and feed water salinity. The distillate output rate decreased with salt by approximately 1kg/h distillate per 10 g/kg of feed water salinity. An energetic optimum that shifts with salinity was detected for moduleoperation. Modules with different membrane surface areas have been characterised. The benefit of an increase in active membrane area is a significant reduction in the specific energy demand, rather than the increase of the distillate output rate. The benefit of an increase in membrane area is less, using higher feed water salinities. Upcoming considerations need to encounter economic factors in order to find a more optimized module configuration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To prove functionality and quality of the spiral wound module technology, investigations on distillate quality have been performed. Throughout all experiments presented in this work, distillate conductivity did not exceed /DC4 dist =3.5 𝛍 S/cm, which is considered very low. In a preliminary experiment, using a emulated double-stage process configuration, high purity distillate with a conductivity as low as /DC4 dist =0.19 𝛍 S/cm can be produced.
